data.title: Block to pay $40M to New York state over Cash App anti-money laundering failures
data.description:
Block (NYSE:XYZ) will pay a $40M penalty for failures in its Bank Secrecy Act/Anti-Money Laundering compliance program, the New York State Superintendent of Financial Services Adrienne A. Harris said on Thursday.

In addition to the penalty, Block (NYSE:XYZ) is required to hire an independent monitor to evaluate its compliance with the department's regulations and its remediation efforts.

The department's investigation found that Block's (XYZ) BSA/AML program had "critical gaps," including customer due diligence, failure to implement sufficient risk-based controls designed to prevent money laundering and illicit activity, and failure to effectively and timely monitor transaction.

Specifically, the company's tax treatment of high-risk bitcoin (BTC-USD) transactions allowed largely anonymous transactions to proceed without proper scrutiny, the NYDFS said.

"The rapid growth of Block’s Cash App absent a robust compliance function created risk and vulnerabilities that violated the rules financial services companies operating in New York must adhere to," Harris said.

Block (XYZ) stock dropped 3.0% in Thursday morning trading.

data.title: Northern Technologies projects second half growth supported by oil and gas investments and China market recovery
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Northern Technologies International Corporation (NTIC) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Patrick Lynch highlighted the challenges faced during Q2 2025, including "unprecedented changes in U.S. trade and economic policies" and the seasonality of their industrial and oil and gas business. He emphasized optimism for a rebound in the second half of the fiscal year, driven by Natur-Tec and Zerust Oil and Gas sales, citing demand from new and existing customers.
  • Total consolidated net sales for the quarter were $19.1 million, an 8.5% decrease year-over-year. Specific business segments saw declines: Zerust Oil and Gas (-28.5%), Natur-Tec (-11.8%), and Zerust Industrial (-3.7%).
  • Joint venture sales fell by 15.7% year-over-year to $19.8 million, influenced by high energy prices and regional economic pressures, particularly in Europe.
  • CFO Matthew Wolsfeld noted that operating expenses for the quarter increased 2.4% year-over-year to $8.8 million, attributed to investments in the oil and gas sales team and higher personnel costs. He also highlighted a 440 basis point decline in gross profit margin, now at 35.6%, due to a less profitable sales mix.
  • NTIC reported net income of $434,000, or $0.04 per diluted share, compared to $1.7 million, or $0.17 per diluted share, in Q2 2024.

Outlook

  • Management indicated expectations of growth in the second half of fiscal 2025, supported by strategic investments in the oil and gas sector and anticipated rebounds in Natur-Tec and Zerust Oil and Gas sales.
  • CEO Lynch mentioned a significant new opportunity with a large U.S. distributor for Natur-Tec solutions and a new technology line for food packaging. Trial results for this initiative are promising and could significantly impact future sales if successful.
  • NTIC anticipates continued stabilization and growth in the Chinese market, with expectations for China to become a significant geographic market.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated net sales for Q2 2025 were $19.1 million. Net income was $434,000, or $0.04 per diluted share, with non-GAAP adjusted income showing a loss of $300,000, or $0.03 per share.
  • Cash and cash equivalents totaled $5.1 million as of February 28, 2025, with $13 million held at international joint ventures.
  • Operating cash flows for the first six months of fiscal 2025 reached $3.2 million. Outstanding debt increased to $8.1 million, up from $7.3 million as of November 30, 2024.

Q&A

  • Timothy Clarkson, Van Clemens, asked about recent investments in the oil and gas sales team. CEO Lynch noted eight hires, of which some have left, but he expects the impact of these investments to materialize in the second half of the fiscal year.
  • Clarkson further inquired about Natur-Tec opportunities. Lynch mentioned a large U.S. distributor and a new food packaging technology as key growth drivers.
  • Gus Richard, Northland Capital Markets, asked about gross margin pressures. CFO Wolsfeld explained increased competition in the Natur-Tec segment, leading to price reductions of 4-8%, as well as reduced revenue in the oil and gas segment.
  • Gregory Weaver, Invicta Capital Management, sought clarification on the anticipated second-half ramp in oil and gas sales. CEO Lynch emphasized growth potential in oil storage tank and pipeline casing protection, supported by investments in sales and technical personnel across key regions.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns over revenue declines, gross margin pressures, and the effectiveness of recent investments. Questions focused on timelines for sales ramp-ups and the impact of competitive pressures on margins.
  • Management maintained a cautiously optimistic tone, emphasizing strategic investments and growth potential in their key business segments. CEO Lynch and CFO Wolsfeld consistently expressed confidence in second-half growth prospects, supported by phrases such as "we believe" and "we are well positioned."

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 2025 saw a sequential decline in net sales compared to Q1 2025, from $21.3 million to $19.1 million. This was attributed to seasonality and timing of orders.
  • Gross profit margin declined from 38.3% in Q1 2025 to 35.6% in Q2 2025, driven by competitive pricing pressures in Natur-Tec and a less favorable sales mix.
  • Operating expenses decreased sequentially from $9.5 million in Q1 2025 to $8.8 million in Q2 2025, reflecting cost management efforts.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged risks from "unprecedented changes in U.S. trade and economic policies" and high energy prices affecting European joint ventures.
  • Competitive pricing pressures in the Natur-Tec segment were highlighted as a significant challenge, with price reductions of up to 8% implemented to maintain market share.
  • Seasonal and order timing impacts continue to pose risks to quarterly performance.

Final Takeaway

Northern Technologies International Corporation is navigating a challenging economic environment with a focus on strategic investments in its oil and gas and Natur-Tec segments. While Q2 results reflect headwinds, management remains optimistic about second-half growth, supported by a growing sales pipeline, new market opportunities, and stabilization in key geographies such as China. Investors will closely monitor the impact of these initiatives on the company’s financial performance in the coming quarters.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: White House fielding tariff offers from 15 countries, NEC chief Hassett says
data.description:
The White House is fielding offers on tariff deals with 15 countries and is closing in on pacts with some of those governments, National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett said Thursday. 

Hassett’s remarks came a day after President Donald Trump unexpectedly paused reciprocal tariffs for 90 days on more than 75 countries that he announced on April 2. However, increased the levy on China-produced goods. 

“We’ve got a few deals that we’ve been working on ahead of this that are really, really well advanced,” Hassett told CNBC. He did not name the countries involved in discussions before Trump’s Wednesday announcement. 

“There's a big inventory of deals that are right close to the finish line,” he said. 

He later told reporters that the US Trade Representative informed him that the administration has received proposals from “about 15” countries” and officials are “studying and considering” if “they’re good enough to present to the president,” Reuters reported. 

Hassett added that there will be a high-level meeting on Thursday to discuss how to proceed. 

The Wall Street Journal reported late Wednesday that Trump has acknowledged privately that his trade policies could cause a recession, but he wants to ensure they don’t trigger a depression, citing people familiar with the conversations. 

S&P 500 Funds: (MUTF:FXAIX), (MUTF:VFIAX), (MUTF:VFFSX), (MUTF:SWPPX), (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS) and (NYSEARCA:SPXU). 

China ETFs: (NYSEARCA:KWEB), (NASDAQ:PGJ), (NYSEARCA:CQQQ), (NYSEARCA:FXI), (NYSEARCA:GXC), (NASDAQ:MCHI), (NYSEARCA:FLCH), (BATS:CNYA), (NYSEARCA:ASHR), and (NYSEARCA:YINN).

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Fed's Bowman sets banking supervision goals in vice chair nomination hearing
data.description:
Federal Reserve Governor Michelle Bowman outlined Thursday what she will prioritize as the central bank's new vice chair for supervision, if confirmed by the U.S. Senate.

"If confirmed, I will prioritize reforming and refocusing supervision, restoring regulatory tailoring, ensuring a viable path for innovation in the banking system, and promoting transparency and accountability," according to her prepared testimony before the U.S. Senate Banking Committee for her nomination hearing.

She stressed bank supervision must be overhauled and redirected toward addressing material financial risk "to ensure that the U.S. banking system is safe and sound."

If the Senate confirms her, Bowman is likely to favor a less stringent approach to bank regulations versus Michael Barr, her predecessor. She had pushed back on a sweeping effort introduced two years ago aimed at requiring banks to beef up their capital buffers. She was selected by President Donald Trump in mid-March.

This is a developing story. Check back for updates.

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, Dow drop as tariff concerns still weigh, CPI comes in cooler
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages fell on Thursday as tariff worries lingered, while the CPI came in cooler than expected in March.

Early on and the S&P 500 (SP500) was -2.5%, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -3%, and the Dow (DJI) was -2%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) dippde 1 basis point to 4.31%. At the same time the 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 9 basis points to 3.81%.

The Consumer Price Index came in at -0.1% M/M vs. +0.1% consensus, +2.4% Y/Y vs. +2.6% consensus, and +2.8% prior.

Core CPI came in at +0.1% M/M vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.2% prior, +2.8% Y/Y vs. +3.0% consensus and +3.1% prior

It was nothing short of a historic day for markets on Wednesday, as U.S. President Donald Trump's pause on reciprocal tariffs against most countries except China sent Wall Street to its best day since the financial crisis of 2008. The S&P 500 rocketed +9.52%; the Nasdaq Composite exploded +12.16%, and the Dow surged +7.87% on Wednesday. 

"Market turmoil turned into elation yesterday as Trump announced a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for countries other than China, which marked a sizable backing off from last week’s tariff escalation," Deutsche Bank's Peter Sidorov said.

The European Union paused countermeasures on U.S. tariffs.

Trump on Wednesday said he would issue a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for more than 75 countries who had reached out to the U.S. for trade negotiations.

Perhaps most crucially, we are currently still on course for a disorderly economic decoupling between the world’s two largest economies, with no immediate signs of either the U.S. or China backing down, Sidorov said.

Trump also upped the tariff rate against China to 125%. 

Recession risk in the US is lower than yesterday but remains high, UBS' Paul Donovan said.

Initial jobless claims edged up to 223K in the past week.

The Fed balance sheet will also land on Thursday.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: KMX, DXCM, X, and more
data.description:
Stock futures dipped on Thursday as investors weighed President Trump’s 90-day tariff reprieve for select countries, which notably excluded China. The U.S. raised tariffs on Chinese goods to 125% on Wednesday, with Trump citing a “lack of respect” following Beijing’s retaliatory actions, intensifying trade war fears. Meanwhile, a cooler-than-expected CPI reading for March offered some relief on the inflation front.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • DexCom (DXCM) +5% – Shares gained after the company announced FDA clearance for its Dexcom G7 15 Day continuous glucose monitoring system for adults with diabetes. The device, offering up to 15.5 days of use, is now the longest-lasting CGM system on the market and boasts a leading accuracy with a MARD of 8.0%. DexCom plans to launch the G7 15 Day in the U.S. during the second half of 2025.
  • Trump Media (NASDAQ:DJT) +7% – Shares extended gains Thursday morning after a double-digit surge Wednesday, when the stock jumped as much as 22.4%, ranking among the top gainers in the communications services sector. The rally followed a Truth Social post by Donald Trump stating, “THIS IS A GREAT TIME TO BUY!!! DJT,” just hours before he announced a 90-day pause on the reciprocal tariffs he unveiled earlier this month. Volume topped 14.45M shares, more than twice its 3-month average. Short interest stands at 5.47%. Year to date, DJT is down 49%, compared to a 7% drop in the S&P 500 (SP500).

Biggest stock losers

  • Constellation Brands (NYSE:STZ) -4% – Shares slipped despite a Q4 beat, as the company’s FY2026 outlook disappointed investors amid trade policy uncertainty. FY26 EPS guidance of $12.60–$12.90 came in well below the $13.94 consensus, while organic net sales are expected to range from -2% to +1%. The company also cut its FY27–FY28 sales growth forecast to flat to +3% (from +1% to +3%). Constellation targets FY26 operating cash flow of $2.7–$2.8B and free cash flow of $1.5–$1.6B. It also plans to divest its mainstream wine brands to The Wine Group and will review its organizational structure, aiming for over $200M in annualized cost savings by FY28.
  • U.S. Steel (NYSE:X) -11% – Shares plunged after President Donald Trump reiterated his opposition to the company's acquisition by Japan’s Nippon Steel, despite previously suggesting openness to a minority stake. “We love Japan, but U.S. Steel is a special company,” he stated. The stock had earlier surged to a 52-week intraday high of $46.18 following Trump’s call for a national security review of the deal, but momentum reversed sharply on renewed takeover concerns.
  • Easterly Government Properties (DEA) -6% – Shares declined after the company announced a 32% cut to its quarterly dividend, lowering it to $0.18 per share from $0.265. The board also approved a 1-for-2.5 reverse stock split, set to take effect on April 28, 2025, under which shareholders will receive one share for every 2.5 held. Post-split, the dividend will adjust to $0.45 per share. CEO Darrell Crate noted the changes aim to align payout ratios with cash flow and free up capital to support the firm's expanding development pipeline.
  • CarMax (KMX) -18% – Shares tumbled after the used car retailer’s Q4 profit surged 81% Y/Y but still missed Street expectations. EPS came in at $0.58 vs. $0.66 consensus, despite solid performance driven by higher unit sales, gross profit, and CAF income. Revenue rose 6.7% Y/Y to $6B, slightly topping estimates. However, profit per vehicle slipped 3.8% to $1,045, which weighed on sentiment. For FY2026, CarMax plans to open six new locations and four reconditioning/auction centers, with capital expenditures projected at $575M.

data.title: Novavax in selloff after RFK Jr.’s comments on COVID vaccine
data.description:
• Novavax (NASDAQ:NVAX) shares sold off on Thursday after Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. cast doubts about the efficacy of the company's COVID shot amid a delay for its U.S. approval.
  • Early this month, The Wall Street Journal reported that after the resignation of its chief vaccine regulator, Dr. Peter Marks, the FDA has missed a key deadline to grant full approval for NVAX’s shot, currently allowed for use under the emergency use authorization.
  • During an interview with Dr. Jon Lapook of CBS News, RFK Jr. attributed the delay to the vaccine’s single-antigen composition, which he said has never worked against respiratory diseases.
  • “We’re looking at that vaccine, and it is a single-antigen vaccine. And, for respiratory illnesses, the single-antigen vaccines have never worked,” RFK Jr. said, adding that “we are actually shifting our priorities to multiple-antigen vaccines. And NIH is already working on a number of those.”

data.title: DoorDash partners with Coco Robotics to offer sidewalk delivery
data.description:
DoorDash (NASDAQ:DASH) will begin offering sidewalk robot delivery in select U.S. cities through an expansion of its partnership with food delivery robotics company, Coco Robotics.

The rollout between DoorDash (NASDAQ:DASH) and Coco Robotics is currently live in Los Angeles and Chicago, where DoorDash (NASDAQ:DASH) customers can have their orders delivered by a fleet of emissions-free sidewalk robots.

“Not every delivery needs a 2-ton car just to deliver two chicken sandwiches,” said Harrison Shih, Senior Director of DoorDash Labs. “We believe the future of delivery will be multi-modal, and we’re thrilled to partner with Coco to expand sidewalk robot deliveries that complement the Dasher network as we continue to enhance the DoorDash experience for customers and merchants.”

The expansion into the U.S. builds on an existing relationship between the two companies in Helsinki, Finland, where Coco robots have been delivering for Wolt – DoorDash’s international arm – since the beginning of this year. Coco robots have already completed 100K deliveries for Wolt during the pilot phase.

By expanding delivery options, DoorDash (NASDAQ:DASH) strives to achieve the most efficient delivery methods using dashers, drones, and autonomous robots.

Press Release

data.title: Payoneer Global acquires China-based payment service provider
data.description:
Fintech Payoneer Global (NASDAQ:PAYO) announced on Wednesday the close of the acquisition of a licensed China-based payment service provider, Easylink Payment.

The company had announced the receipt of regulatory approvals in China related to the acquisition on February 13.

data.title: Ford, Lear, Visteon downgraded at Goldman Sachs on tariff risks, weaker demand
data.description:
Goldman Sachs analysts on Thursday downgraded their outlook for the U.S. automotive and industrial technology sectors, citing growing concerns over global competition, weakening consumer demand and rising costs from tariffs.

The bank issued multiple rating changes and estimate revisions across the space, reflecting what it called a “more difficult cyclical dynamic” for the remainder of 2025 and beyond.

Ford downgraded on tariff exposure, weaker margins

Among the most notable changes, Ford Motor (NYSE:F) was cut to Neutral from Buy. Goldman acknowledged that its earlier bullish stance, initiated in September 2024, was overly optimistic.

Since then, the Wall Street consensus estimate for 2025 earnings per share has been revised down by 32% and the stock is down 10%, the analysts wrote, noting that the broader sector has fared even worse. Ford’s (NYSE:F) relative resilience was attributed to investor sentiment at the time of the previous upgrade and cost-cutting progress.

Still, the analysts flagged growing global competition, reduced consumer appetite and higher tariff burdens as key risks. While Ford’s (F) U.S. manufacturing footprint may help buffer against some tariff fallout, Goldman said valuation expansion compared with traditional peers and a slower-than-expected ramp in software and services are reasons for concern.

GM maintains Buy; Tesla and Rivian still Neutral

Goldman is sticking with its Buy rating on General Motors (NYSE:GM), citing its more attractive valuation versus Ford (F) and slightly lower expected tariff-related EPS impact. Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) and Rivian (NASDAQ:RIVN) remain Neutral-rated, with the bank weighing risks from consumer softness and policy uncertainty against long-term opportunities in AI and vehicle architecture licensing, respectively.

Tier-1 suppliers hit hard: Lear, Visteon downgraded

The bank also downgraded auto suppliers Lear (NYSE:LEA) and Visteon (NASDAQ:VC) to Neutral from Buy, pointing to the sector's inability to fully offset lower industry volumes. Both companies face among the highest exposure to tariffs as a percentage of earnings before interest and taxes, which could significantly compress margins.

Goldman maintained Sell ratings on other suppliers with downside risk or structural headwinds, including Magna (NYSE:MGA), Luminar (NASDAQ:LAZR), QuantumScape (NYSE:QS) and ChargePoint (NYSE:CHPT).

Selective bright spots: BorgWarner, Sensata, Aurora

Despite the broader pessimism, Goldman upgraded a few select names:

  • BorgWarner (NYSE:BWA) was raised to Buy from Neutral, with analysts pointing to its favorable China exposure, in-region manufacturing strategy, and potential growth across all powertrain types, especially in EV content and hybrid solutions.
  • Sensata Technologies (NYSE:ST) was also upgraded to Buy, following a sharp 24% year-to-date selloff. Goldman called current valuation levels a trough, even on below-consensus earnings, and sees a path to EPS growth in 2026 and 2027 as industrial demand recovers and vehicle electrification gains pace.
  • Aurora Innovation (NASDAQ:AUR) received a bump to Neutral from Sell, with analysts noting that their previous bearish catalyst — slower autonomous trucking ramp — has largely played out. Aurora (NASDAQ:AUR) stock has surged 99% since being placed on the Sell list in January, despite revenue downgrades. Goldman now sees a scarcity premium and long-term investor interest in autonomy as supporting the stock.

Aurora is seen as a first-mover in autonomous trucking and is expected to begin driverless commercial operations this year, albeit on a small scale, the analysts wrote, while cautioning that the company still faces a multiyear path to profitability and will likely need additional capital.

Outlook: Tariff drag, valuation pressures

Goldman’s reset on the industry reflects deepening concerns across the U.S. auto complex, especially as new tariffs add cost pressures and consumer sentiment deteriorates. The bank's analysts suggest investors should expect ongoing estimate cuts and tighter margins, but also see selective opportunities emerging in names with tariff insulation, flexible cost structures and clear long-term positioning.
